items.q: Why are infrared cameras used to detect warm objects in the dark?
items.correct: Warm objects emit infrared radiation due to their temperature; the camera detects this emission.
items.misconception: Infrared cameras shine infrared light onto objects so they reflect back visibly.
items.random_wrong: Infrared cameras detect heat moving through the air around objects.
items.why: Students assume cameras always work by reflection (like visible-light cameras), missing that thermal cameras passively detect emitted IR.

items.q: Which has the longer wavelength: microwaves or radio waves?
items.correct: Radio waves — they sit at the longest-wavelength end of the EM spectrum.
items.misconception: Microwaves, because they are used in 'big' devices like radar.
items.random_wrong: They have the same wavelength because both are used for communication.
items.why: Students remember 'micro' as 'small' and assume the wavelength is the smallest, missing that 'micro' refers historically to the wavelength relative to radio, not to absolute size.

items.q: Why do atoms emit light only at specific wavelengths rather than a continuous spectrum?
items.correct: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels; transitions between levels emit photons of specific frequencies (E = hf).
items.misconception: Atoms can emit any wavelength, but only certain ones are visible.
items.random_wrong: Atoms emit only one colour each, determined by their temperature.
items.why: Students don't internalise the quantisation of energy levels; without it, line spectra look arbitrary instead of a direct consequence of quantum mechanics.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: Correct answer: 5 km. “7 km” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: Correct answer: Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
